question: A patient with bilateral buccal and lingual expansion of the posterior mandibles has undergone biopsy and surgical exploration. Based on the imaging, which surgical procedure is most appropriate?

options.A: Exploration with curettage of cavity walls
options.B: En bloc resection with segmental mandibulectomy
options.E: Immediate bone grafting with rigid fixation

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the panoramic image for lesion location and radiographic behavior. The radiograph shows bilateral, fairly well‑demarcated radiolucent areas in the posterior mandibles (right and left), appearing largely unilocular with smooth/scalloped margins and thinning but preservation of cortical outlines; there are no obvious ill‑defined borders, cortical destruction, or aggressive periosteal changes.

Next, I relate these imaging features to the clinical question, which asks which surgical procedure is most appropriate for these posterior mandibular lesions with buccal and lingual expansion. The imaging is most consistent with a benign, intraosseous cystic process rather than a destructive or infiltrative lesion.

Then, I consider how each option aligns with those observations. En bloc resection with segmental mandibulectomy (B) or peripheral ostectomy/marginal mandibulectomy (D) are radical procedures appropriate for aggressive or neoplastic disease and are not supported by the well‑defined, nonaggressive appearance here. Marsupialization with long‑term decompression (C) and immediate bone grafting with rigid fixation (E) are strategies used for very large cysts or when major bony defects are expected after resection; the current images do not show such extensive bone loss. Exploration with curettage of the cavity walls (A) is a limited, tissue‑sparing surgical approach that is appropriate for well‑circumscribed cystic lesions to elicit bleeding and promote bone healing.

In summary, given the bilateral, well‑defined, nonaggressive radiolucencies confined to the posterior mandibles, a conservative surgical exploration with curettage of the cavity walls best matches the imaging and is the most appropriate choice.
caption: Radiographic and histopathological photographs of our case of co-occurrence of florid cemento-osseous dysplasia with two simple bone cysts. (A) Panoramic radiograph showing a well-defined unilocular and a well-demarcated multilocular radiolucent lesions with a sclerotic border and involving the periapical areas of teeth 44 to 46 and teeth 35 to 37, respectively. A mixed radiolucent and radiopaque lesion at the periapical area of tooth 26 and two separate radiopaque masses at the periapical areas of the teeth 41 and 31, respectively, were also found. (B, C and D) Microphotographs exhibiting a strip of bone tissue and a band of endosteum-like tissue composed of a sheet of osteoblasts in an osteoid matrix (Hematoxylin and eosin stain; original magnification; B, 4 × , C, 10 × and D, 20 ×). (E, F and G) Microphotographs demonstrating a long band-like tissue composed of relatively dense or loose vascular fibrous connective tissues with cholesterol slits surrounded by foreign body giant cells and a lymphoplasma cell and histiocyte infiltrate (Hematoxylin and eosin stain; original magnification; E, 4 × , F and D, 20 ×).
sub_caption: (A) Panoramic radiograph showing a well-defined unilocular and a well-demarcated multilocular radiolucent lesions with a sclerotic border and involving the periapical areas of teeth 44 to 46 and teeth 35 to 37, respectively. A mixed radiolucent and radiopaque lesion at the periapical area of tooth 26 and two separate radiopaque masses at the periapical areas of the teeth 41 and 31, respectively, were also found.
image_context: This 48-year-old female patient was referred to the dental department of our hospital for treatment of a large radiolucent lesion at the right posterior mandible by a local dental clinic. The patient complained of a painful sensation at the right mandibular first molar which received endodontic treatment before and was covered by a temporary filling at the occlusal surface. The patient had no systemic disease and no any food or drug allergy. Intraoral examination revealed buccal and lingual expansion at both the right and left posterior mandibular regions. The panoramic radiography revealed a well-defined unilocular and a well-demarcated multilocular radiolucent lesions with a sclerotic border and involving the periapical areas of teeth 44 to 46 and teeth 35 to 37, respectively (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>A). The superior aspect of the radiolucent lesions demonstrated a scalloped margin between the roots of teeth, highly suggestive of two simple bone cysts at the bilateral posterior mandibular regions (A). The superior aspect of the radiolucent lesions demonstrated a scalloped margin between the roots of teeth, highly suggestive of two simple bone cysts at the bilateral posterior mandibular regions (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>A). Electric pulp test showed the tooth 36 with a normal response value (24/80) and the tooth 37 with an abnormally high response value (71/80) compared to other teeth. A mixed radiolucent and radiopaque lesion at the periapical area of tooth 26 and two separate radiopaque masses at the periapical areas of the teeth 41 and 31, respectively, were also found (A). Electric pulp test showed the tooth 36 with a normal response value (24/80) and the tooth 37 with an abnormally high response value (71/80) compared to other teeth. A mixed radiolucent and radiopaque lesion at the periapical area of tooth 26 and two separate radiopaque masses at the periapical areas of the teeth 41 and 31, respectively, were also found (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>A). Cone-beam computed tomography showed bone expansion at the bilateral posterior mandibular regions (data not shown). For obtaining correct histopathological diagnoses, two biopsy procedures were performed at the lingual side of the two mandibular radiolucent lesions. Both radiolucent lesions revealed an empty cavity with some clear straw-colored fluid at the bottom on surgical exploration during the biopsy procedure. Microscopic examination of the small thin and long specimen taken from the inner lining of the right mandibular bone cavity showed a strip of bone tissue and a band of endosteum-like tissue composed of a sheet of osteoblasts in an osteoid matrix, compatible with the lining wall tissue of a simple bone cyst (A). Cone-beam computed tomography showed bone expansion at the bilateral posterior mandibular regions (data not shown). For obtaining correct histopathological diagnoses, two biopsy procedures were performed at the lingual side of the two mandibular radiolucent lesions. Both radiolucent lesions revealed an empty cavity with some clear straw-colored fluid at the bottom on surgical exploration during the biopsy procedure. Microscopic examination of the small thin and long specimen taken from the inner lining of the right mandibular bone cavity showed a strip of bone tissue and a band of endosteum-like tissue composed of a sheet of osteoblasts in an osteoid matrix, compatible with the lining wall tissue of a simple bone cyst (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>B, C and D). Another tissue specimen taken from the multilocular lesion at the left posterior mandible exhibited a long band-like tissue composed of relatively dense or loose vascular fibrous connective tissues with cholesterol slits surrounded by foreign body giant cells and a lymphoplasma cell and histiocyte infiltrate, also highly suggestive of a simple bone cyst (B, C and D). Another tissue specimen taken from the multilocular lesion at the left posterior mandible exhibited a long band-like tissue composed of relatively dense or loose vascular fibrous connective tissues with cholesterol slits surrounded by foreign body giant cells and a lymphoplasma cell and histiocyte infiltrate, also highly suggestive of a simple bone cyst (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>E, F and G). Taken the whole radiographic and histopathological findings together, a clinical diagnosis of florid cemento-osseous dysplasia with concomitant occurrences of two simple bone cysts was confirmed.E, F and G). Taken the whole radiographic and histopathological findings together, a clinical diagnosis of florid cemento-osseous dysplasia with concomitant occurrences of two simple bone cysts was confirmed.Figure 1Radiographic and histopathological photographs of our case of co-occurrence of florid cemento-osseous dysplasia with two simple bone cysts. (A) Panoramic radiograph showing a well-defined unilocular and a well-demarcated multilocular radiolucent lesions with a sclerotic border and involving the periapical areas of teeth 44 to 46 and teeth 35 to 37, respectively. A mixed radiolucent and radiopaque lesion at the periapical area of tooth 26 and two separate radiopaque masses at the periapical areas of the teeth 41 and 31, respectively, were also found. (B, C and D) Microphotographs exhibiting a strip of bone tissue and a band of endosteum-like tissue composed of a sheet of osteoblasts in an osteoid matrix (Hematoxylin and eosin stain; original magnification; B, 4 × , C, 10 × and D, 20 ×). (E, F and G) Microphotographs demonstrating a long band-like tissue composed of relatively dense or loose vascular fibrous connective tissues with cholesterol slits surrounded by foreign body giant cells and a lymphoplasma cell and histiocyte infiltrate (Hematoxylin and eosin stain; original magnification; E, 4 × , F and D, 20 ×).Fig. 1
relevant_image_context: Intraoral examination revealed buccal and lingual expansion at both the right and left posterior mandibular regions. The panoramic radiography revealed a well-defined unilocular and a well-demarcated multilocular radiolucent lesions with a sclerotic border and involving the periapical areas of teeth 44 to 46 and teeth 35 to 37, respectively (Fig. 1 A). The superior aspect of the radiolucent lesions demonstrated a scalloped margin between the roots of teeth, highly suggestive of two simple bone cysts at the bilateral posterior mandibular regions (A). A mixed radiolucent and radiopaque lesion at the periapical area of tooth 26 and two separate radiopaque masses at the periapical areas of the teeth 41 and 31, respectively, were also found (A). Cone-beam computed tomography showed bone expansion at the bilateral posterior mandibular regions (data not shown). For obtaining correct histopathological diagnoses, two biopsy procedures were performed at the lingual side of the two mandibular radiolucent lesions. Both radiolucent lesions revealed an empty cavity with some clear straw-colored fluid at the bottom on surgical exploration during the biopsy procedure. Microscopic examination of the small thin and long specimen taken from the inner lining of the right mandibular bone cavity showed a strip of bone tissue and a band of endosteum-like tissue composed of a sheet of osteoblasts in an osteoid matrix, compatible with the lining wall tissue of a simple bone cyst (A).
